episode_title: Essentials: The Biology of Taste Perception & Sugar Craving | Dr. Charles Zuker
summary: Dr. Charles Zuker discusses the neurobiology of taste perception, distinguishing between detection (sensory receptor activation) and perception (brain interpretation). He explains how the five basic tastes (sweet, sour, bitter, salty, umami) map onto specific neural circuits from taste receptors through the brainstem to the cortex. A central finding is that sugar preference is driven by a gut-brain axis mechanism: while artificial sweeteners activate taste receptors like sugar, they fail to activate intestinal sensors that recognize glucose, preventing satisfaction of sugar cravings and contributing to overconsumption. He argues obesity is fundamentally a brain circuit disorder rather than a metabolism problem, with implications for understanding how highly processed foods exploit evolved neural systems for nutrient recognition.
science_topics: Taste receptor molecular biology and signal transduction
Neural circuits for taste perception from tongue to gustatory cortex
Gut-brain axis and vagal signaling in nutrient sensing
Post-ingestive nutrient recognition by intestinal cells
Desensitization mechanisms at receptor and neural circuit levels
Taste plasticity and learning (e.g., coffee acceptance despite initial bitterness)
Homeostatic modulation of taste by internal physiological state
Artificial sweeteners and glucose-specific intestinal sensors
Neural basis of appetitive versus aversive taste responses
Sugar and fat as hijackers of evolved nutrient-sensing circuits
claims_to_note: Artificial sweeteners do not activate gut-brain axis receptors for glucose, leaving sugar cravings unsatisfied despite taste activation
Obesity is primarily a disease of brain circuits rather than metabolism or physiology
Five basic tastes (sweet, sour, bitter, salty, umami) accommodate all dietary needs
Bitter taste is enriched at back of tongue as last-line defense against toxins
Mice lacking sweet taste receptors can still develop sugar preference through post-ingestive learning via gut-brain axis
Taste preferences can be modified by learning and internal metabolic state despite hardwired initial valences
Modern highly processed foods co-opt nutrient-sensing circuits in ways that would not occur in nature
Each taste quality maps to distinct brain regions in gustatory cortex
Salt preference switches from appetitive to aversive at high concentrations unless animal is salt-deprived
policy_relevance: Potential implications for food labeling and artificial sweetener regulation based on mechanistic understanding of their ineffectiveness at satisfying sugar cravings
Brain-based perspective on obesity may inform public health approaches focusing on neural circuit interventions rather than solely caloric restriction or metabolic targets
Gap between training of metabolic scientists and neuroscientists acknowledged as potential policy concern for addressing nutrition and obesity
Mechanistic understanding of gut-brain axis could inform design of dietary interventions and appetite regulation strategies
key_quotes: The brain is only made of neurons that only understand electrical signals. So how do you transform that reality into nothing but electrical signals that now need to represent the world? That process is what we can operationally define as perception.
I don't think obesity is a disease of metabolism. I believe obesity is a disease of brain circuits. The brain ultimately appears to be the conductor of this orchestra of physiology and metabolism.

Sugar is recognized normally by the tongue, activates an appetitive response. Now you ingest it and it activates a selective group of cells in your intestines that send a signal to the brain via the vagal ganglia that says 'I got what I need.' Artificial sweeteners never satisfy the craving for sugar because they don't activate these gut cells.

Quantum Science Concepts in Enhancing Sensing and Imaging Technologies: Applications for Biology: Proceedings of a Workshop
publications.description: Quantum concepts hold the potential to enable significant advances in sensing and imaging technologies that could be vital to the study of biological systems. The workshop Quantum Science Concepts in Enhancing Sensing and Imaging Technologies: Applications for Biology, held online March 8-10, 2021, was organized to examine the research and development needs to advance biological applications of quantum technology. Hosted by the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, the event brought together experts working on state-of-the-art, quantum-enabled technologies and scientists who are interested in applying these technologies to biological systems. Through talks, panels, and discussions, the workshop facilitated a better understanding of the current and future biological applications of quantum-enabled technologies in fields such as microbiology, molecular biology, cell biology, plant science, mycology, and many others. Public Engagement on Genetically Modified Organisms: When Science and Citizens Connect: Workshop Summary
publications.description: The National Research Council's Roundtable on Public Interfaces of the Life Sciences held a 2-day workshop on January 15-16, 2015, in Washington, DC to explore the public interfaces between scientists and citizens in the context of genetically engineered (GE) organisms. The workshop presentations and discussions dealt with perspectives on scientific engagement in a world where science is interpreted through a variety of lenses, including cultural values and political dispositions, and with strategies based on evidence in social science to improve public conversation about controversial topics in science. The workshop focused on public perceptions and debates about genetically engineered plants and animals, commonly known as genetically modified organisms (GMOs), because the development and application of GMOs are heavily debated among some stakeholders, including scientists. publications.title: Facial Recognition Technology: Current Capabilities, Future Prospects, and Governance
publications.description: Facial recognition technology is increasingly used for identity verification and identification, from aiding law enforcement investigations to identifying potential security threats at large venues. However, advances in this technology have outpaced laws and regulations, raising significant concerns related to equity, privacy, and civil liberties. This report explores the current capabilities, future possibilities, and necessary governance for facial recognition technology. Facial Recognition Technology discusses legal, societal, and ethical implications of the technology, and recommends ways that federal agencies and others developing and deploying the technology can mitigate potential harms and enact more comprehensive safeguards.

publications.title: Nutrient Requirements of Dogs and Cats
publications.description: Updating recommendations last made by the National Research Council in the mid-1980s, this report provides nutrient recommendations based on physical activity and stage in life, major factors that influence nutrient needs. It looks at how nutrients are metabolized in the bodies of dogs and cats, indications of nutrient deficiency, and diseases related to poor nutrition. The report provides a valuable resource for industry professionals formulating diets, scientists setting research agendas, government officials developing regulations for pet food labeling, and as a university textbook for dog and cat nutrition. It can also guide pet owners feeding decisions for their pets with information on specific nutrient needs, characteristics of different types of pet foods, and factors to consider when feeding cats and dogs.
publications.committee: Board on Agriculture and Natural Resources

publications.title: Nutrient Requirements of Dairy Cattle: Eighth Revised Edition
publications.description: Since 1944, the National Research Council (NRC) has published seven editions of the Nutrient Requirements of Dairy Cattle. This reference has guided nutritionists and other professionals in academia and the dairy and feed industries in developing and implementing nutritional and feeding programs for dairy cattle. The eighth revised edition of the Nutrient Requirements of Dairy Cattle builds on the previous editions. A great deal of new research has been published and there is a large amount of new information for many nutrients. This book represents a comprehensive review of the most recent information available on efficient, profitable, and environmentally conscious dairy cattle nutrition and ingredient composition.

Reflecting Sunlight: Recommendations for Solar Geoengineering Research and Research Governance
publications.description: The National Research Council report Climate Intervention: Reflecting Sunlight to Cool Earth (NRC, 2015) reviewed the state of the science and provided high-level findings and recommendations regarding SG methods. This current study was tasked to update the 2015 assessment of the state of understanding and to provide recommendations for how to establish a research program, what to encompass in the research agenda, and what mechanisms to employ for governing this research.
publications.committee: Committee on Science, Technology, and Law
